Carboxylic acid (COOH).description: A carbon oxoacid acid carrying at least one ‒C(=O)OH group and having the structure RC(=O)OH, where R is any any monovalent functional group. Carboxylic acids are the most common type of organic acid.

Ester.description: A compound formally derived from an oxoacid RkE(=O)l(OH)m (l > 0) and an alcohol, phenol, heteroarenol, or enol by linking with formal loss of water from an acidic hydroxy group of the former and a hydroxy group of the latter.

Ether (C-O-C).description: An organooxygen compound with formula ROR, where R is not hydrogen.

Aromatic ring.description: Any benzenoid aromatic compound consisting of the benzene skeleton and its substituted derivatives.

Amide (CONH).description: An amide of a carboxylic acid, having the structure RC(=O)NR2. The term is used as a suffix in systematic name formation to denote the ‒C(=O)NH2 group including its carbon atom.

Amine (NH2).description: A compound formally derived from ammonia by replacing one, two or three hydrogen atoms by hydrocarbyl groups.

Carbonyl (C=O).description: A functional group consisting of a carbon atom doubly bonded to an oxygen atom (C=O). Found in aldehydes, ketones, carboxylic acids, esters, and amides.

Hydroxyl (OH).description: A functional group consisting of an oxygen atom bonded to a hydrogen atom (‒OH). Present in alcohols and phenols; confers hydrogen-bond donor capability.

Halogen.description: A compound containing at least one carbon-halogen bond (where X is a halogen atom).

Nitrile (CN).description: A compound having the structure RC≡N; thus a C-substituted derivative of hydrocyanic acid, HC≡N. In systematic nomenclature, the suffix nitrile denotes the triply bound ≡N atom, not the carbon atom attached to it.

Nitro (NO2).description: A nitro compound having the nitro group (‒NO2) attached to a carbon atom.

Sulfonamide.description: An amide of a sulfonic acid RS(=O)2NR'2.

Imidazole.description: A five-membered organic heterocycle containing two nitrogen atoms at positions 1 and 3, or any of its derivatives; compounds containing an imidazole skeleton.

Benzene.description: A six-carbon aromatic annulene in which each carbon atom donates one of its two 2p electrons into a delocalised π system. A toxic, flammable liquid byproduct of coal distillation, it is used as an industrial solvent. Benzene is a carcinogen that also damages bone marrow and the central nervous system.

Tertiary amine.description: A compound formally derived from ammonia by replacing three hydrogen atoms by hydrocarbyl groups.
